content: A credit default swap (CDS) is a derivative that transfers credit risk from one party to another. The protection buyer pays periodic premiums to the seller; if a credit event occurs (default, bankruptcy, restructuring), the seller compensates the buyer for losses on the reference entity's debt. CDS spreads reflect market-implied default probability—a 100 basis point spread suggests roughly 1-2% annual default risk. CDS enabled investors to speculate on or hedge credit risk without owning the underlying bonds. During the 2008 crisis, CDS on mortgage-backed securities amplified losses when AIG couldn't meet obligations. Post-crisis reforms mandated central clearing for standardized CDS to reduce counterparty risk.
